(Correct Option 1). A client who has undergone colectomy is an appropriate candidate because postoperative clients often experience significant pain and are typically alert and able to use PCA devices. PCA allows effective and timely pain control in such surgical cases.

(Correct Option 2). A client with acute pancreatitis is a candidate because this condition causes severe abdominal pain. PCA provides consistent and controlled pain relief, and these clients are usually cognitively intact.

(Correct Option 3). A client who has undergone gastrectomy is also appropriate for PCA use due to postoperative pain management needs. If the client is alert and oriented, PCA is safe and effective.

(Option 4). A client with renal insufficiency is incorrect because opioid medications may accumulate due to decreased renal clearance, increasing the risk of toxicity. PCA must be used cautiously or avoided depending on the severity of renal impairment.

(Option 5). A client with Alzheimer’s disease is incorrect because cognitive impairment prevents safe use of PCA. These clients may not understand how or when to administer medication, increasing the risk of harm.

Educational Objective
PCA is best suited for clients who are alert, oriented, and able to follow instructions, such as postoperative or acute pain patients. It should be avoided in clients with cognitive impairment or conditions that increase the risk of medication accumulation.
questionList.subject: Pharmacology
questionList.title: Patient-Controlled Analgesia

answerChoiceList.choice: A client who has undergone colectomy
A client who has undergone gastrectomy

questionList.questionText: A nurse is preparing a list of measures that will help promote sleep. Identify the measures that would be included on the list. Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Sleep hygiene refers to a set of practices and environmental factors that are conducive to promoting quality and restful sleep. Proper sleep hygiene helps regulate the circadian rhythm, which is the body's internal clock controlling sleep-wake cycles. Factors such as consistent sleep schedules, a comfortable environment, and minimizing stimulants play a crucial role. Stimulants like caffeine and alcohol can disrupt sleep patterns, while excessive activity before bedtime can increase alertness and delay sleep onset. Maintaining a quiet, dark, and comfortable environment supports relaxation and improves sleep quality.

(Correct Option 4). Adjusting the room temperature to a comfortable level promotes sleep by creating an environment conducive to relaxation. A comfortable temperature helps regulate body physiology and supports uninterrupted sleep.

(Correct Option 5). Eliminating lights, noise, and distractions is essential for good sleep hygiene. A quiet and dark environment enhances melatonin secretion and helps maintain continuous, restful sleep.

(Correct Option 6). Getting up at the same time each day and avoiding naps helps regulate the circadian rhythm. This consistency improves sleep quality and makes it easier to fall asleep at night.

(Option 1). Exercising just before bedtime is incorrect because it can increase alertness and stimulate the nervous system, making it harder to fall asleep. Exercise should be done earlier in the day.

(Option 2). Drinking a glass of wine at bedtime is incorrect because although alcohol may initially induce sleep, it disrupts sleep cycles and leads to poor-quality sleep.

(Option 3). Drinking black tea before bedtime is incorrect because it contains caffeine, a stimulant that can interfere with falling asleep and maintaining sleep.

Educational Objective
Effective sleep hygiene includes maintaining a consistent sleep schedule, creating a quiet and comfortable environment, and avoiding stimulants or disruptive habits before bedtime to promote restful sleep.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Sleep Hygiene

answerChoiceList.choice: Drink a glass of wine at bedtime
Drink a cup of black tea before bedtime
Adjust the room temperature to a comfortable level
Eliminate lights, noise, and other environmental distractions
Get up at the same time each day and avoid naps during the day

questionList.questionText: A client who experienced a brain attack (stroke) is experiencing residual dysphagia. Which of the following foods would the nurse remove from the client’s meal tray?
questionList.explanationText:
Dysphagia is a swallowing disorder commonly seen after a stroke (brain attack), caused by impairment in the muscles and nerves responsible for swallowing. This condition increases the risk of aspiration, which can lead to aspiration pneumonia. Clients with dysphagia require foods that are soft, moist, and easy to swallow, such as pureed or thickened foods. Foods that are dry, hard, crumbly, or contain small particles are dangerous because they are difficult to control in the mouth and may easily be aspirated. Proper dietary modifications are essential to ensure safe swallowing and adequate nutrition.

(Correct Option 1). Peas should be removed because they are small, round, and easily aspirated. These foods are difficult to control during swallowing and increase the risk of choking and aspiration in clients with dysphagia.

(Option 2). Scrambled eggs are incorrect because they are soft, moist, and easy to swallow. They are commonly recommended for clients with dysphagia as they pose minimal risk of aspiration.

(Option 3). Cheese casserole is incorrect because it is typically soft and cohesive, making it easier to swallow compared to foods with small particles or dry textures.

(Option 4). Mashed potatoes are incorrect because they are smooth and soft, which makes them safe and appropriate for clients with swallowing difficulties.

Educational Objective
Clients with dysphagia should avoid foods that are small, hard, or difficult to control, such as peas, and instead consume soft, moist, and easily swallowable foods to reduce the risk of aspiration.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Dysphagia Management

questionList.questionText: A client is resuming eating after undergoing partial gastrectomy. What does the nurse tell the client to avoid doing as a means of minimizing the risk of complications?
questionList.explanationText:
Partial gastrectomy involves surgical removal of a portion of the stomach, which can lead to complications such as dumping syndrome. Dumping syndrome occurs when food, especially high-sugar or liquid-heavy meals, moves too quickly from the stomach into the small intestine. This rapid gastric emptying causes symptoms like nausea, diarrhea, abdominal cramping, dizziness, and tachycardia. To minimize these complications, clients are advised to follow dietary modifications such as eating small, frequent meals, consuming high-protein, low-carbohydrate foods, and avoiding the intake of liquids during meals. Liquids can accelerate gastric emptying, worsening symptoms.

(Correct Option 3). Drinking liquids with meals should be avoided because it increases the rate of gastric emptying, contributing to dumping syndrome. Clients should instead drink fluids between meals to reduce the risk of complications.

(Option 1). Lying down after eating is incorrect because this action is actually recommended for clients after gastrectomy. It helps slow gastric emptying and reduces symptoms of dumping syndrome.

(Option 2). Eating high-protein foods is incorrect because a high-protein diet is beneficial after gastrectomy. Protein helps maintain nutritional status and slows gastric emptying.

(Option 4). Eating six small meals per day is incorrect because this is a recommended dietary strategy. Small, frequent meals help prevent overloading the stomach and reduce the risk of dumping syndrome.

Educational Objective
After partial gastrectomy, clients should avoid drinking liquids with meals to prevent dumping syndrome. Instead, they should consume small, frequent meals and maintain a high-protein diet for optimal recovery.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Post-Gastrectomy Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Eating six small meals per day

questionList.questionText: A regular diet has been prescribed for a client with a leg fracture who has been placed in skeletal traction. Which foods that will promote wound healing does the nurse encourage the client to select from the hospital menu?
questionList.explanationText:
Wound healing is a complex physiological process that requires adequate intake of protein, vitamins (especially vitamin C), minerals, and calories. Protein is essential for tissue repair and collagen synthesis, while vitamin C supports collagen formation and immune function. Additionally, nutrients such as calcium and vitamin D are important for bone healing in clients with fractures. A balanced diet rich in lean proteins, fruits, vegetables, and dairy products enhances recovery and prevents complications such as delayed healing or infection. Foods lacking these nutrients may impair the healing process.

(Correct Option 3). Chicken breast, broccoli, strawberries, and milk provide a high-protein, nutrient-rich combination. Chicken offers lean protein, broccoli and strawberries are rich in vitamin C, and milk provides calcium and vitamin D, all of which are essential for wound and bone healing.

(Option 1). Spare ribs, rice, gelatin, and tea are incorrect because although ribs contain protein, they are high in fat and lack sufficient vitamins needed for healing. Gelatin and tea offer minimal nutritional value.

(Option 2). Pasta, garlic bread, and ginger ale are incorrect because they are primarily carbohydrate-rich and low in protein and essential nutrients. This combination does not adequately support tissue repair.

(Option 4). Peanut butter and jelly sandwich, chocolate cake, and tea are incorrect because they are high in sugar and low in essential nutrients. While peanut butter contains some protein, the overall meal lacks the necessary vitamins and minerals for optimal healing.

Educational Objective
Optimal wound healing requires a diet rich in protein, vitamin C, calcium, and essential nutrients. Nurses should encourage clients to select balanced meals with lean proteins, fruits, vegetables, and dairy products.
questionList.subject: Nutrition
questionList.title: Wound Healing Nutrition

answerChoiceList.choice: Peanut butter and jelly sandwich, chocolate cake, tea

questionList.questionText: For which vitamin deficiency should the nurse monitor the client who is on a vegan diet?
questionList.explanationText:
Vegan diets exclude all animal products, including meat, dairy, and eggs. While these diets can be nutritionally adequate, they may lead to deficiencies in certain nutrients that are primarily found in animal sources. One of the most important is vitamin B12 (cobalamin), which is essential for red blood cell formation, neurological function, and DNA synthesis. Vitamin B12 is naturally found almost exclusively in animal-based foods, making vegans particularly at risk for deficiency. A lack of vitamin B12 can result in megaloblastic anemia, fatigue, and neurological symptoms such as numbness and tingling. Therefore, monitoring and supplementation are often necessary.

(Correct Option 2). Vitamin B12 deficiency is the primary concern in clients following a vegan diet because it is not readily available in plant-based foods. Without supplementation or fortified foods, deficiency can develop, leading to anemia and neurological complications.

(Option 1). Vitamin A is incorrect because it can be obtained from plant sources in the form of beta-carotene, which the body converts into vitamin A. Therefore, deficiency is less likely in a well-balanced vegan diet.

(Option 3). Vitamin C is incorrect because it is abundant in fruits and vegetables, which are staple components of a vegan diet. Deficiency is uncommon in vegans.

(Option 4). Vitamin E is incorrect because it is found in plant oils, nuts, and seeds, all of which are commonly consumed in vegan diets.

Educational Objective
Clients on vegan diets are at risk for vitamin B12 deficiency due to the absence of animal products. Monitoring and supplementation are essential to prevent anemia and neurological complications.
questionList.subject: Nutrition
questionList.title: Vitamin Deficiencies

questionList.questionText: A nurse has taught the client with a herniated lumbar disk about proper body mechanics and other information about low back care. The nurse determines that the client needs further instruction if the client says:
questionList.explanationText:
A herniated lumbar disk occurs when the inner nucleus of the intervertebral disk protrudes through the outer layer, placing pressure on nearby spinal nerves. This condition causes pain, inflammation, and possible nerve compression. Proper body mechanics are essential to reduce strain on the spine and prevent worsening of the condition. Key principles include bending at the knees instead of the waist, avoiding twisting motions, maintaining proper alignment, and using the log-rolling technique when getting out of bed. Strengthening exercises and a healthy diet that prevents constipation are also important, as straining can increase intra-abdominal pressure and worsen back pain.

(Correct Option 4). Saying "I should get out of bed by sitting up straight and swinging my legs over the side of the bed" indicates a need for further instruction because this movement involves twisting and strain on the spine. The correct technique is the log-rolling method, where the client rolls onto the side and then pushes up with the arms while keeping the spine aligned.

(Option 1). Bending at the knees to pick things up is correct because it reduces strain on the lower back and distributes weight more safely through the legs.

(Option 2). Increasing fiber and fluids is correct because it helps prevent constipation and straining, which can worsen back pain and spinal pressure.

(Option 3). Strengthening back muscles through swimming or walking is correct because these are low-impact exercises that improve muscle support and reduce stress on the spine.

Educational Objective
Clients with herniated disks should use proper body mechanics, including bending at the knees and using the log-rolling technique when getting out of bed, to prevent further spinal injury and reduce pain.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Back Care and Body Mechanics

answerChoiceList.choice: “I should bend at the knees to pick things up.”
“I need to increase the fiber and fluids in my diet.”
“I can strengthen my back muscles by swimming or walking.”
“I should get out of bed by sitting up straight and swinging my legs over the side of the bed.”

questionList.questionText: A client has been told to apply cold packs to a knee injury, and the client asks the nurse how this will help the injury. The nurse explains that a cold pack:
questionList.explanationText:
Cold therapy (cryotherapy) is commonly used in the management of acute injuries such as sprains, strains, and trauma. The application of cold causes vasoconstriction, which leads to a reduction in blood flow to the affected area. This helps decrease swelling, inflammation, and pain by limiting the accumulation of fluid and inflammatory mediators. Additionally, cold therapy slows nerve conduction, which contributes to pain relief. It is most effective when applied shortly after injury to minimize tissue damage and edema formation.

(Correct Option 4). Cold packs reduce blood flow to the extremity by causing vasoconstriction. This action limits swelling and inflammation, making it beneficial in the acute phase of injury management.

(Option 1). Reducing muscle tension is incorrect because this is more commonly associated with heat therapy, which promotes muscle relaxation and increased blood flow.

(Option 2). Dilating the blood vessels is incorrect because cold therapy causes vasoconstriction, not vasodilation. Vasodilation is an effect of heat application.

(Option 3). Promoting muscle relaxation is incorrect because this is primarily an effect of warm or heat therapy, not cold therapy.

Educational Objective
Cold therapy works by causing vasoconstriction, which reduces blood flow, swelling, and inflammation. It is most effective in the acute phase of injury management.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Cold Therapy

answerChoiceList.choice: Reduces blood flow to the extremity

questionList.questionText: A nurse is evaluating the client’s use of a cane for left-sided weakness. The nurse determines that the client is using the cane incorrectly if the client:
questionList.explanationText:
Proper cane use is essential for clients with unilateral weakness to maintain balance, stability, and safety during ambulation. The cane should always be held on the stronger (unaffected) side to provide maximum support and reduce strain on the weaker limb. During ambulation, the cane moves simultaneously with the weaker leg, allowing weight to be distributed evenly and preventing falls. The client should lean slightly on the cane when advancing the weak leg, and the cane should be positioned about 6 inches to the side of the foot to provide a stable base of support. Incorrect technique can increase the risk of instability, improper weight distribution, and falls.

(Correct Option 2). Moving the cane when the right (strong) leg is moved is incorrect because the cane should move with the weaker (left) leg. This ensures proper weight distribution and balance. Using the cane with the wrong leg disrupts coordination and increases fall risk.

(Option 1). Holding the cane on the right side is correct because the cane should be placed on the stronger side to support the weaker limb and maintain stability.

(Option 3). Leaning on the cane when the right leg moves forward is correct because the client uses the cane to support body weight while advancing the weaker side, ensuring balance.

(Option 4). Keeping the cane 6 inches out to the side of the right foot is correct because this positioning provides a stable base of support and improves balance during ambulation.

Educational Objective
For proper cane use, the cane should be held on the strong side and moved with the weak leg to promote stability, proper weight distribution, and prevent falls.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Assistive Devices

answerChoiceList.choice: Holds the cane on the right side
Moves the cane when the right leg is moved
Leans on the cane when the right leg moves forward
Keeps the cane 6 inches out to the side of the right foot

questionList.questionText: A nurse develops a plan of care for a postoperative client who is receiving intravenous morphine sulfate every 4 hours as needed for pain. Which priority intervention does the nurse include in the plan?
questionList.explanationText:
Postoperative clients receiving opioid analgesics such as morphine sulfate are at increased risk for respiratory depression, decreased respiratory effort, and accumulation of secretions. Morphine depresses the central nervous system (CNS), leading to reduced respiratory rate and depth, which can cause atelectasis and pneumonia if not managed properly. Encouraging coughing and deep breathing helps expand the lungs, mobilize secretions, and improve oxygenation. This intervention is critical in preventing postoperative pulmonary complications, which are a major priority in nursing care. Therefore, maintaining adequate ventilation and airway clearance is essential for patient safety.

(Correct Option 3). Encouraging coughing and deep breathing is the priority because it helps prevent respiratory complications associated with opioid use and immobility. This intervention promotes lung expansion, clears secretions, and reduces the risk of pneumonia and atelectasis.

(Option 1). Encouraging oral fluid intake is important for hydration but is not the priority intervention compared to maintaining adequate respiratory function in a postoperative client receiving opioids.

(Option 2). Maintaining the client in a supine position is incorrect because it can actually impair lung expansion and increase the risk of respiratory complications. Semi-Fowler’s or upright positions are preferred.

(Option 4). Administering morphine sulfate around the clock is incorrect because the prescription is as needed (PRN). Routine administration without assessment may increase the risk of overdose and respiratory depression.

Educational Objective
Postoperative clients receiving opioids are at risk for respiratory depression. Encouraging coughing and deep breathing is a priority intervention to prevent atelectasis and pneumonia and maintain adequate oxygenation.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Postoperative Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Maintaining the client in a supine position
Administering the morphine sulfate around the clock

questionList.questionText: Triamterene (Dyrenium) has been prescribed for a client with a history of hypertension. Which fruits should the nurse tell the client to avoid while taking this medication? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Triamterene (Dyrenium) is a potassium-sparing diuretic used in the management of hypertension and edema. Unlike other diuretics, it works by reducing sodium reabsorption while retaining potassium in the body. This mechanism places the client at risk for hyperkalemia, a condition characterized by elevated potassium levels that can lead to cardiac arrhythmias, muscle weakness, and life-threatening complications. Therefore, clients taking potassium-sparing diuretics must avoid foods that are high in potassium, including certain fruits. Monitoring dietary intake is crucial to prevent dangerous electrolyte imbalances.

(Correct Option 1, 4, 5). Prunes, avocados, and nectarines are high in potassium and should be avoided when taking triamterene. Consuming these fruits can increase the risk of hyperkalemia, which may lead to serious cardiac complications.

(Option 2). Apples are incorrect because they are relatively low in potassium and are generally safe to consume while taking potassium-sparing diuretics.

(Option 3). Peaches are incorrect because they contain moderate to low potassium levels and are not typically restricted in this situation.

(Option 6). Cranberries are incorrect because they are low in potassium and do not significantly contribute to hyperkalemia risk.

Educational Objective
Clients taking potassium-sparing diuretics like triamterene should avoid high-potassium foods to prevent hyperkalemia and associated cardiac complications.
questionList.subject: Pharmacology
questionList.title: Diuretics

questionList.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who has a fever and is diaphoretic. The nurse monitors the client’s urinary output and laboratory values, anticipating that the client’s:
questionList.explanationText:
Fever and diaphoresis (excessive sweating) result in significant fluid loss through the skin, leading to dehydration if fluid intake does not adequately replace these losses. As the body loses fluid, the kidneys respond by conserving water to maintain circulating blood volume and blood pressure. This is mediated by hormones such as antidiuretic hormone (ADH), which increases water reabsorption in the kidneys. Consequently, urine output decreases, and the urine becomes more concentrated, leading to increased urine specific gravity and increased serum osmolality. These compensatory mechanisms are essential to prevent severe fluid imbalance and maintain homeostasis.

(Correct Option 1). Urine output will be decreased because the body is conserving fluid in response to dehydration caused by fever and diaphoresis. Increased ADH secretion promotes water reabsorption, reducing urine volume.

(Option 2). Urine production will be increased is incorrect because fluid loss leads to decreased, not increased, urine output as the body attempts to conserve water.

(Option 3). Serum osmolality will be decreased is incorrect because dehydration causes increased serum osmolality due to a higher concentration of solutes in the blood.

(Option 4). Urine specific gravity will be decreased is incorrect because the urine becomes more concentrated, resulting in an increased urine specific gravity.

Educational Objective
Fever and diaphoresis lead to fluid loss and dehydration, causing decreased urine output, increased urine concentration, and increased serum osmolality as the body conserves water.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Fluid and Electrolyte Balance

answerChoiceList.choice: Urine specific gravity will be decreased

questionList.questionText: A nurse is providing dietary instructions to a client with tuberculosis. Which of the following foods would the nurse specifically instruct the client to include more of in the daily diet?
questionList.explanationText:
Tuberculosis (TB) is a chronic infectious disease that leads to increased metabolic demands, tissue breakdown, and weight loss. Clients with TB often experience fatigue, anorexia, and malnutrition, which can impair immune function and delay recovery. Therefore, nutritional therapy focuses on a high-protein, high-calorie diet to promote tissue repair and immune support. Additionally, vitamin C-rich foods are essential because they enhance immune function and collagen synthesis. Adequate intake of proteins and vitamins helps improve healing, maintain body weight, and support the body’s defense against infection.

(Correct Option 4). Meats and citrus fruits are ideal because meats provide high-quality protein आवश्यक for tissue repair, while citrus fruits are rich in vitamin C, which boosts immune function and aids recovery from infection.

(Option 1). Rice and fish are incorrect because although fish provides protein, rice is primarily a carbohydrate source and does not supply the additional vitamins needed for optimal immune support.

(Option 2). Eggs and bacon are incorrect because while they provide protein, bacon is high in fat and not nutritionally balanced, and this option lacks essential vitamins like vitamin C.

(Option 3). Cereals and broccoli are incorrect because although broccoli provides vitamins, cereals are mainly carbohydrates and may lack sufficient protein needed for tissue repair in TB.

Educational Objective
Clients with tuberculosis require a high-protein, high-calorie diet with vitamin C to support immune function, promote healing, and prevent weight loss.
questionList.subject: Nutrition
questionList.title: Therapeutic Diets

questionList.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client whose urine output was 25 mL for 2 consecutive hours. Which of the following client-related factors does the nurse recognize as increasing blood flow to the kidneys?
questionList.explanationText:
Renal blood flow is a critical factor in maintaining adequate glomerular filtration rate (GFR) and urine output. The kidneys rely on sufficient perfusion to filter blood and produce urine. Certain physiological responses, such as sympathetic nervous system activation, stress, and catecholamine release (e.g., norepinephrine), cause vasoconstriction, reducing renal blood flow and decreasing urine output. In contrast, dopamine at low doses promotes renal vasodilation, increasing blood flow to the kidneys and improving urine production. Understanding these mechanisms is essential for managing conditions associated with oliguria and impaired renal perfusion.

(Correct Option 2). The release of dopamine increases blood flow to the kidneys by causing renal vasodilation. This enhances perfusion, improves glomerular filtration, and can increase urine output, making it beneficial in clients with reduced renal function.

(Option 1). Physiological stress is incorrect because it activates the sympathetic nervous system, leading to vasoconstriction and decreased renal blood flow.

(Option 3). Release of norepinephrine is incorrect because it causes vasoconstriction, which reduces renal perfusion and decreases urine output.

(Option 4). Sympathetic nervous system stimulation is incorrect because it leads to vasoconstriction of renal blood vessels, reducing blood flow to the kidneys.

Educational Objective
Dopamine promotes renal vasodilation and increases blood flow to the kidneys, whereas stress and sympathetic stimulation decrease renal perfusion and urine output.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Renal Perfusion

questionList.questionText: A home care nurse makes a visit to a new mother who delivered a 7-lb girl 72 hours ago. The mother tells the nurse that her newborn seems to sleep almost all day. The nurse most appropriately responds by telling the mother that:
questionList.explanationText:
Newborn sleep patterns are an important aspect of early neonatal development and often cause concern for new parents. Healthy full-term newborns typically sleep between 14 to 18 hours per day, with an average of about 16 hours daily. This sleep is usually intermittent, occurring in short cycles due to frequent needs for feeding and care. The high amount of sleep supports brain development, growth, and energy conservation. It is considered normal behavior unless accompanied by signs such as poor feeding, lethargy beyond normal arousal, or abnormal neurological responses. Therefore, educating parents about normal sleep expectations is essential to reduce anxiety and promote appropriate newborn care.

(Correct Option 1). Telling the mother that most newborns sleep about 16 hours a day is correct because this is within the normal range of newborn sleep patterns. Providing this reassurance helps reduce parental anxiety and supports understanding of normal neonatal behavior.

(Option 2). Suggesting that the baby should be checked by a doctor is incorrect because excessive sleeping alone, without other abnormal signs, is normal for newborns and does not require immediate medical evaluation.

(Option 3). Advising the mother to watch for neurological alterations is unnecessary at this stage because the newborn’s behavior is within normal limits, and no concerning signs have been reported.

(Option 4). Recommending waking the baby every hour for stimulation is incorrect because newborns require adequate rest for growth and development, and unnecessary stimulation may disrupt normal sleep patterns.

Educational Objective
Healthy newborns typically sleep about 16 hours per day. Nurses should reassure parents that this is normal and only advise further evaluation if abnormal symptoms are present.
questionList.subject: Maternal-Newborn Nursing
questionList.title: Newborn Care

answerChoiceList.choice: “Most newborns sleep about 16 hours a day”
“We should probably have the baby checked out by the doctor.”
“If you see any other neurological alterations, call the physician.”
“It’s important to wake the baby every hour to provide stimulation.”

questionList.questionText: A client arrives at the emergency department after sustaining an ankle injury, and the physician prescribes the application of a cold compress to the ankle. The nurse, preparing to apply the compress, assesses the ankle and notes that it is extremely edematous. The nurse should:
questionList.explanationText:
Cold therapy (cryotherapy) is commonly used to reduce inflammation, pain, and edema by causing vasoconstriction, which limits blood flow to the injured area. However, when extreme edema is present, it may indicate severe tissue injury, compartment syndrome risk, or impaired circulation. Applying cold therapy without further assessment can potentially worsen tissue perfusion and delay healing. The nurse must always prioritize patient safety, reassessment findings, and clinical judgment before implementing prescribed interventions. In such cases, clarification of the prescription is necessary to prevent complications.

(Correct Option 2). The presence of extreme edema is a significant abnormal finding that requires further evaluation. The nurse should consult with the physician before applying the cold compress to ensure that the treatment is still appropriate and safe, especially considering possible complications like circulatory compromise or compartment syndrome.

(Option 1). Applying the cold compress immediately without addressing the severe edema ignores a critical assessment finding. This could lead to inadequate circulation or worsening of tissue damage, making it an unsafe action.

(Option 3). Alternating between cold and hot compresses is not appropriate in the acute phase of injury. Heat causes vasodilation, which can increase swelling and inflammation, especially when significant edema is already present.

(Option 4). While elevation and cold application are generally appropriate for edema, the severity of the swelling requires further medical evaluation first. Proceeding without consulting the physician could be unsafe.

Educational Objective
When abnormal or severe assessment findings (such as extreme edema) are present, the nurse must question or clarify prescriptions before implementation. Patient safety and clinical judgment take priority over routine interventions.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Cold and Heat Therapy

answerChoiceList.choice: Apply the cold compress to the ankle
Consult with the physician before applying the cold compress
Apply the cold compress for 20 minutes, and then apply a hot compress for 20 minutes
Elevate the ankle and place cold compresses under and on top of the ankle

questionList.questionText: A nurse has provided dietary instructions to a client with a new diagnosis of gout. Which menu suggestions by the client indicate to the nurse that the client needs additional instruction? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Gout is a metabolic disorder characterized by the accumulation of uric acid crystals in joints due to hyperuricemia. Uric acid is a byproduct of purine metabolism, and foods high in purines can increase uric acid levels, leading to acute gout attacks. Clients with gout are advised to avoid organ meats, certain seafood, and high-purine foods, while encouraging intake of low-purine foods such as vegetables, fruits, and grains. Dietary management plays a crucial role in preventing exacerbations and maintaining optimal uric acid levels.

(Correct Option 3 and Option 5). Scallops and chicken liver are high in purines, which can significantly increase uric acid production and trigger gout attacks. These food choices indicate that the client does not fully understand dietary restrictions and requires additional teaching.

(Option 1). Carrots are low in purines and are considered safe for clients with gout. This indicates correct understanding of dietary recommendations.

(Option 2). Tapioca is also low in purines and is a safe carbohydrate choice, making it appropriate for a gout-friendly diet.

(Option 4). Broccoli is a low-purine vegetable and is safe for consumption. Although it contains small amounts of purines, it does not significantly contribute to hyperuricemia.

Educational Objective
Clients with gout should avoid high-purine foods such as organ meats and certain seafood. Emphasis should be placed on consuming low-purine foods to prevent uric acid buildup and reduce the risk of gout attacks.
questionList.subject: Nutrition
questionList.title: Gout Diet Management

questionList.questionText: A nurse is administering an enema to a client. While the enema solution is being instilled, the client complains of abdominal cramping. The nurse should:
questionList.explanationText:
Enema administration involves introducing fluid into the rectum to promote bowel evacuation. During instillation, the flow of solution can stimulate intestinal peristalsis, which may lead to abdominal cramping. This cramping is often caused by rapid infusion or excessive pressure from the enema solution. The nurse must regulate the flow rate to ensure client comfort and safety, as excessive discomfort can lead to premature expulsion of the solution and ineffective treatment. Proper technique includes adjusting the height of the enema bag and controlling the flow to minimize irritation to the intestinal mucosa.

(Correct Option 1). When the client experiences abdominal cramping, the nurse should temporarily clamp the tubing to stop the flow of solution. This allows the bowel to relax and reduces discomfort. Once the cramping subsides, the nurse can resume instillation at a slower rate to ensure tolerance.

(Option 2). Removing the enema tube is unnecessary unless the client cannot tolerate the procedure at all. Mild cramping is expected and can be managed by adjusting the flow rate rather than discontinuing the procedure completely.

(Option 3). Stopping the instillation and allowing the client to expel the solution prematurely prevents adequate retention time, making the enema ineffective. The goal is to help the client retain the solution for therapeutic benefit.

(Option 4). Raising the enema bag increases the flow rate and pressure, which will worsen cramping and discomfort. This action is unsafe and contraindicated.

Educational Objective
During enema administration, if a client experiences cramping, the nurse should slow or temporarily stop the flow by clamping the tubing. This promotes comfort and allows effective retention of the solution.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Enema Administration

answerChoiceList.choice: Remove the enema tube and allow the client to rest
Stop the instillation and allow the client to expel the solution
Raise the enema bag to quickly finish instillation of the solution

questionList.questionText: A nurse administers an oil retention enema to a client. Afterward, the nurse instructs the client to:
questionList.explanationText:
Oil retention enemas are used to lubricate the rectum and soften hardened stool, making defecation easier and less painful. Unlike cleansing enemas, which stimulate rapid bowel evacuation, oil retention enemas require the solution to remain in the rectum for an extended period to allow absorption and stool softening. The oil coats the fecal mass and intestinal lining, reducing friction and facilitating smoother passage. Proper instruction is essential to ensure therapeutic effectiveness, as premature expulsion will prevent the oil from adequately softening the stool.

(Correct Option 2). The client should be instructed to retain the enema for several hours, if possible. This allows sufficient time for the oil to penetrate and soften the stool, ensuring effective bowel movement with minimal discomfort.

(Option 1). Immediately expelling the enema defeats its purpose because the oil will not have enough time to soften the stool. This makes the intervention ineffective.

(Option 3). Oil retention enemas do not produce immediate bowel movements. Expecting defecation within 30 minutes is incorrect because the action is slow and gradual.

(Option 4). Oil retention enemas are designed to minimize irritation and cramping. Unlike cleansing enemas, they are generally gentle and should not cause significant discomfort.

Educational Objective
Oil retention enemas must be retained for several hours to soften stool effectively. Proper retention is key to achieving the desired therapeutic outcome.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Enema Administration

answerChoiceList.choice: Retain the enema for several hours
Expect to experience cramping induced by the solution

questionList.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client with cirrhosis. As part of the teaching regarding dietary means of minimizing the effects of the disorder, the nurse educates the client about foods that are high in thiamine. The nurse determines that the client has the best understanding of the material if the client states to increase the intake of which of the following? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Cirrhosis is a chronic liver disease characterized by fibrosis and impaired liver function, which affects the metabolism and storage of essential nutrients. Clients with cirrhosis are often at risk for vitamin deficiencies, particularly thiamine (vitamin B1), due to poor intake, impaired absorption, and altered metabolism. Thiamine is crucial for carbohydrate metabolism, nerve function, and energy production. Foods rich in thiamine include whole grains, legumes, nuts, and certain vegetables. Adequate intake helps prevent complications such as Wernicke’s encephalopathy and neurological dysfunction, which are common in chronic liver disease.

(Correct Option 2, Option 5, and Option 6). Peanuts, asparagus, and whole-grain cereals are good sources of thiamine. Increasing intake of these foods demonstrates correct understanding of dietary management for cirrhosis and prevention of vitamin B1 deficiency.

(Option 1). Milk is not a significant source of thiamine. While it provides other nutrients such as calcium and protein, it does not contribute substantially to thiamine intake.

(Option 3). Chicken contains moderate amounts of thiamine, but it is not considered one of the best or primary dietary sources compared to whole grains and legumes.

(Option 4). Broccoli contains small amounts of thiamine but is not a rich source. It is beneficial for overall nutrition but not specifically for increasing thiamine intake.

Educational Objective
Clients with cirrhosis should increase intake of thiamine-rich foods such as nuts, legumes, vegetables like asparagus, and whole grains to prevent deficiency and support neurological function.
questionList.subject: Nutrition
questionList.title: Cirrhosis Nutrition

questionList.questionText: A nurse provides instructions to a client about preventing injury while using crutches. The nurse tells the client to avoid resting the underside of the arm on the crutch pad, mainly because it could result in:
questionList.explanationText:
Crutch walking requires proper technique to prevent complications and ensure safety. The axillary (underarm) region contains important neurovascular structures, including the brachial plexus, which is responsible for nerve supply to the upper limb. Resting body weight on the crutch pads places excessive pressure on these nerves, leading to a condition known as crutch palsy. This can result in nerve compression, numbness, tingling, and even temporary or permanent motor dysfunction. Therefore, weight should be supported by the hands and arms, not the axilla, to avoid nerve injury.

(Correct Option 2). Resting the underside of the arm on the crutch pad can cause compression of the brachial plexus nerves, leading to nerve injury. This may result in symptoms such as weakness, numbness, or paralysis in the affected arm, making it the most serious complication to avoid.

(Option 1). Although prolonged pressure could contribute to skin irritation or breakdown, this is not the primary concern compared to the risk of nerve damage.

(Option 3). An abnormal stance may occur with improper crutch use, but it is not specifically related to pressure on the axillary region.

(Option 4). While improper use of crutches can increase the risk of falls, resting on the crutch pads specifically leads to nerve compression, not directly to falls.

Educational Objective
Clients using crutches should avoid bearing weight on the axilla to prevent brachial plexus nerve injury (crutch palsy). Weight should be supported by the hands to ensure safety and proper mobility.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Mobility Aids and Safety

questionList.questionText: A client with liver cancer who is undergoing chemotherapy tells the nurse that some foods on the meal tray taste bitter. Which of the following foods does the nurse suggest that the client avoid, knowing that it is most likely to taste bitter to the client?
questionList.explanationText:
Chemotherapy commonly causes taste alterations (dysgeusia), particularly a bitter or metallic taste. This occurs due to the effects of chemotherapy on taste buds and salivary glands. Foods high in protein, especially red meats, are often perceived as bitter or metallic by patients undergoing treatment. This can lead to decreased appetite, poor nutritional intake, and weight loss. To promote adequate nutrition, nurses should recommend alternative protein sources and foods that are more palatable, such as sweet or bland foods.

(Correct Option 1). Beef is a red meat and is commonly reported to have a bitter or metallic taste in clients receiving chemotherapy. Avoiding beef can help improve food intake and reduce unpleasant taste experiences.

(Option 2). Custard is generally sweet and mild in flavor, making it well tolerated by clients with altered taste perception.

(Option 3). Potatoes are bland and unlikely to produce a bitter taste, making them a suitable food option for clients undergoing chemotherapy.

(Option 4). Cantaloupe is sweet and refreshing, often preferred by clients experiencing taste changes, and does not contribute to a bitter taste.

Educational Objective
Clients receiving chemotherapy often experience bitter or metallic taste changes, especially with red meats. Nurses should recommend avoiding such foods and encourage more palatable alternatives to maintain adequate nutrition.
questionList.subject: Nutrition
questionList.title: Chemotherapy Nutrition

questionList.questionText: A client tells the nurse that he is feeling fatigued because he must get up several times during the night to urinate. The nurse documents that the client is experiencing:
questionList.explanationText:
Urinary elimination patterns provide important clues about a client’s health status. Nocturia refers specifically to waking up during the night to urinate, which can disrupt sleep and lead to fatigue and decreased quality of life. It may be associated with conditions such as benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), urinary tract infections, diabetes, or fluid redistribution. Understanding urinary terms is essential for accurate documentation and communication in clinical practice. Other terms include anuria (absence of urine), oliguria (reduced urine output), and polyuria (excessive urine output), each reflecting different physiological disturbances.

(Correct Option 4). Nocturia is defined as the need to wake up multiple times during the night to urinate. The client’s complaint of interrupted sleep due to frequent nighttime urination directly corresponds to this condition.

(Option 1). Anuria refers to complete absence of urine output, which is not consistent with frequent nighttime urination.

(Option 2). Oliguria is characterized by decreased urine output, typically less than 400 mL/day, and does not involve increased nighttime urination.

(Option 3). Polyuria refers to excessive urine production, but it does not specifically indicate that urination occurs during the night.

Educational Objective
Nocturia is defined as frequent nighttime urination and can lead to sleep disruption and fatigue. Accurate identification of urinary terms is essential for proper assessment and documentation.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Urinary Elimination

questionList.questionText: A nurse notes documentation in a client’s medical record indicating that the client is experiencing oliguria. On the basis of this notation, the nurse determines that the client:
questionList.explanationText:
Oliguria refers to a condition of reduced urine output, typically defined as less than 400 mL per day in adults. It indicates a diminished ability of the kidneys to produce urine, which may result from dehydration, renal impairment, decreased renal perfusion, or obstruction. Understanding urinary terms is essential for accurate clinical assessment. In contrast, anuria means absence of urine, polyuria indicates excessive urine production, and incontinence refers to involuntary leakage. Recognizing oliguria helps the nurse identify potential renal dysfunction or fluid imbalance requiring prompt intervention.

(Correct Option 4). Oliguria means the client has a diminished capacity to form urine, indicating reduced kidney function or decreased urine production. This aligns directly with the definition of oliguria.

(Option 1). Being unable to produce urine describes anuria, not oliguria. Anuria is a more severe condition with nearly complete absence of urine output.

(Option 2). Voiding large amounts of urine refers to polyuria, which is the opposite of oliguria.

(Option 3). Difficulty with leakage of urine describes urinary incontinence, which is unrelated to urine production volume.

Educational Objective
Oliguria is defined as decreased urine output and reflects a diminished ability of the kidneys to produce urine. It is an important sign of potential renal dysfunction or fluid imbalance.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Urinary Elimination

answerChoiceList.choice: Is voiding large amounts of urine
Has difficulty with leakage of urine
Has a diminished capacity to form urine

questionList.questionText: A nurse provides information to a client about the importance of consuming fluids every day. If the client has no renal or cardiac disease or any other disorder requiring fluid alterations, how many milliliters of fluid should the nurse recommend that the client consume each day?
questionList.explanationText:
Fluid balance is essential for maintaining homeostasis, including proper cellular function, circulation, temperature regulation, and waste elimination. In healthy adults without underlying conditions, daily fluid requirements are influenced by metabolic needs, activity level, and environmental factors. The average recommended intake is approximately 2000 to 2500 mL per day, which helps compensate for losses through urine, perspiration, respiration, and feces. Adequate hydration supports kidney function, electrolyte balance, and overall physiological stability.

(Correct Option 4). A daily intake of 2000 to 2500 mL is recommended for healthy adults. This amount ensures sufficient hydration to maintain normal body processes and fluid balance in the absence of any fluid restrictions.

(Option 1). 500 to 1000 mL is insufficient for maintaining hydration and may lead to dehydration and impaired physiological function.

(Option 2). 1000 to 1500 mL is still below the typical daily requirement and may not adequately replace normal fluid losses.

(Option 3). 1500 to 2000 mL is closer to the minimum requirement but may still be inadequate for some individuals depending on activity level and environmental conditions.

Educational Objective
Healthy adults should consume approximately 2000 to 2500 mL of fluids daily to maintain proper hydration, support organ function, and compensate for normal fluid losses.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Fluid Balance

questionList.questionText: A nurse is teaching a client with heart disease about a low-fat diet. Which foods should the nurse tell the client to avoid? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Heart disease is often associated with atherosclerosis, a condition characterized by the buildup of fatty plaques in the arteries. A low-fat diet, particularly one low in saturated fats and cholesterol, is essential to reduce the risk of further cardiovascular complications. Foods high in fat can increase LDL cholesterol levels, contributing to plaque formation and impaired blood flow. Clients are advised to limit intake of high-fat foods such as processed dairy products, certain fruits with high fat content, and salty fatty items, while encouraging consumption of lean proteins, fruits, vegetables, and whole grains.

(Correct Option 1, Option 3, and Option 6). Avocados, green olives, and cream cheese are high in fat content. Although some fats in avocados are healthy, they are still calorie-dense and may need to be limited in a strict low-fat diet. Cream cheese is high in saturated fat, and olives contain significant fat and sodium, making them less suitable for heart disease management.

(Option 2). Baked tuna is a lean protein source and contains omega-3 fatty acids, which are beneficial for heart health and should be encouraged.

(Option 4). Baked potato is low in fat and a good source of carbohydrates and fiber, making it appropriate for a low-fat diet.

(Option 5). Fresh cherries are low in fat and rich in vitamins and antioxidants, making them a healthy choice for clients with heart disease.

Educational Objective
Clients with heart disease should avoid high-fat foods, especially those rich in saturated fats, and instead choose lean proteins, fruits, vegetables, and whole grains to support cardiovascular health.
questionList.subject: Nutrition
questionList.title: Low-Fat Diet

questionList.questionText: A client about to undergo surgery is instructed in postoperative pain relief measures and is asked whether he would like to use a patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) pump. The client asks the nurse to describe the pump. The nurse should tell the client that:
questionList.explanationText:
Patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) is a method of pain management that allows clients to self-administer predetermined doses of analgesic medication, usually opioids, through an intravenous (IV) infusion pump. The PCA device is programmed with specific settings such as dose limits, lockout intervals, and maximum dosage to ensure safety and prevent overdose. This system promotes patient autonomy, timely pain control, and consistent plasma drug levels, reducing delays in pain relief. It is important to note that only the client should activate the PCA button, as administering medication by others increases the risk of respiratory depression and overdose.

(Correct Option 4). The client administers medication by pressing a control button on the PCA pump, allowing for immediate pain relief within safe programmed limits. This ensures that the client receives analgesia only when needed and prevents delays associated with nurse-administered dosing. The built-in safety mechanisms prevent overdose by enforcing lockout intervals.

(Option 1). PCA pumps require an intravenous (IV) line for medication delivery. They do not eliminate the need for IV access; instead, they depend on it for proper function.

(Option 2). PCA dosing is not administered at fixed intervals such as every 4 hours. Instead, the client can self-administer doses as needed within programmed safety limits, making this statement incorrect.

(Option 3). Only the client should press the PCA button. Allowing a spouse or caregiver to administer doses can result in overmedication and serious complications, including respiratory depression.

Educational Objective
PCA pumps allow clients to self-administer pain medication safely by pressing a button, with built-in safeguards such as lockout intervals to prevent overdose. Only the client should control the device.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Patient-Controlled Analgesia

answerChoiceList.choice: The PCA pump eliminates the need for an intravenous (IV) line
The client will be able to deliver his own dose of medication every 4 hours
The client’s spouse will be able to administer medication for the client
The client administers his own medication by pressing a control button
